questions.question: An independent set in a graph is a subset of vertices such that no two vertices in the subset are connected by an edge. An incomplete scheme for a greedy algorithm to find a maximum independent set in a tree is given below: V : Set of all vertices in the tree; I := phi; while V != phi do begin select a vertex u in V such that ________ ; V := V - {u}; if u is such that ________ then I := I union {u} end; Output(I); A. Complete the algorithm by specifying the property of vertex u in each case. B. What is the time complexity of the algorithm?
questions.answer: A. First blank: u is a leaf of the current forest (a vertex of degree <= 1). Second blank: none of u's neighbours is already in I (no neighbour of u has been added to I). B. The algorithm runs in O(n) time on a tree with n vertices.
questions.to_find: Fill the two blanks of the greedy maximum-independent-set algorithm on a tree and state its time complexity

understand.plain: On a tree the maximum independent set can be found greedily: repeatedly pick a leaf, put it into the answer set I, and delete the leaf together with its unique neighbour. The intuition is that a leaf can never hurt you, picking the leaf is always at least as good as picking its only neighbour, so a leaf is always safe to take. To turn the scheme into a correct algorithm, the first blank says select a leaf (degree <= 1) and the second blank says add u only if none of its neighbours is already in I.

keywords.explain: A set of vertices with no edge between any two of them.
keywords.example: In the path a-b-c the set {a, c} is independent because a and c are not adjacent.

keywords.explain: A vertex of degree at most one, a node with a single (or no) edge attached.
keywords.example: In a rooted tree every bottom node with no children is a leaf.

keywords.explain: A locally safe decision (here: always grab a leaf) that provably extends to an optimal global solution.
keywords.example: Taking a leaf into I and removing its neighbour never reduces the maximum independent-set size.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='50' x2='90' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='180' y1='50' x2='270' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='130' x2='40' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='130' x2='140' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='270' y1='130' x2='320' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='180' cy='50' r='11' class='ok'/><circle cx='90' cy='130' r='11' class='bg'/><circle cx='270' cy='130' r='11' class='bg'/><circle cx='40' cy='210' r='11' class='ok'/><circle cx='140' cy='210' r='11' class='ok'/><circle cx='320' cy='210' r='11' class='ok'/><text class='tag mut' x='20' y='30'>green = in I (leaves chosen first)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A small tree where the leaf vertices are chosen into the independent set I first and their parents are skipped, illustrating the greedy leaf-first strategy.

given.aim: State the selection property of u and the inclusion test that make the greedy scheme compute a maximum independent set, and give its running time.

terms.meaning: The current working set of vertices, shrinking as vertices are removed.
terms.example: Initially all n vertices of the tree; one vertex u is deleted each iteration.
terms.connects: The while loop runs while V is non-empty, so it executes O(n) times.

terms.meaning: The independent set being built, initially empty.
terms.example: Leaves are added to I as the loop proceeds.
terms.connects: The output; it ends up being a maximum independent set of the tree.

terms.meaning: The vertex selected each round; the correct property is degree <= 1 in the current forest.
terms.example: A bottom node of the tree with a single edge to its parent.
terms.connects: Choosing a leaf is the safe greedy step that guarantees optimality.

terms.meaning: The total cost of running the algorithm to completion.
terms.example: Each vertex is selected and removed once, with degree maintained in O(1) amortised.
terms.connects: Gives the linear O(n) bound for a tree on n vertices.

given.plan: Recognise the optimal greedy rule for maximum independent set on a tree (always take a leaf, then delete it and its neighbour), translate that into the two blanks, then count the work per vertex to get the running time.

steps.title: First blank: pick a leaf
steps.formula_raw: select u with deg(u) <= 1 (a leaf of the current forest)
steps.apply: The vertex u must be a leaf, that is a vertex of degree at most one in the current graph. A leaf has a single neighbour (its parent), so taking the leaf is never worse than taking that neighbour. Hence the first blank is 'u is a leaf (deg(u) <= 1)'.
steps.note: Picking a leaf is the exchange-argument-safe greedy choice on trees.

steps.title: Second blank: avoid conflicts
steps.formula_raw: add u to I  iff  no neighbour of u is already in I
steps.apply: Add u to the independent set only when including it keeps I independent, i.e. when none of u's neighbours is already in I. Because we always grabbed leaves first, a freshly chosen leaf usually has no neighbour in I and is taken; once a leaf is in I its parent will fail this test and be skipped. So the second blank is 'no neighbour of u is in I'.
steps.note: This guard is exactly the definition of an independent set being preserved.

steps.title: Running time
steps.formula_raw: each vertex handled once  =>  T(n) = O(n)
steps.apply: Every vertex is selected and removed exactly once. Maintaining which vertices are leaves and updating neighbour degrees can be done in O(1) amortised per removal (each edge is touched a constant number of times). With n vertices and n-1 edges in a tree, the total work is O(n).
steps.note: Linear time is the expected complexity for a single-pass tree algorithm.

solution.result: First blank: u is a leaf (degree <= 1). Second blank: no neighbour of u is already in I. Time complexity = O(n).

questions.formula_note: The key idea is the tree greedy rule: a leaf is always safe to take into a maximum independent set, so selecting leaves (first blank) and adding them when conflict-free (second blank) yields an optimal set in O(n) time.

questions.question: The minimum number of edges in a connected cyclic graph on n vertices is: A. n - 1 B. n C. n + 1 D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Fewest edges a connected graph on n vertices can have while still containing a cycle

understand.plain: A connected graph on n vertices needs at least n - 1 edges, and with exactly n - 1 edges it is a tree, which has no cycle. To force a cycle you must add one more edge, which closes a loop. So the smallest connected graph that is cyclic has n edges (a tree plus one extra edge, or a single cycle through all n vertices).

keywords.explain: A graph in which there is a path between every pair of vertices.
keywords.example: A path 1-2-3-4 is connected; two separate edges are not.

keywords.explain: A connected graph with no cycle; on n vertices it has exactly n - 1 edges.
keywords.example: A star with one centre and n - 1 leaves has n - 1 edges and no cycle.

keywords.explain: A closed path that starts and ends at the same vertex using distinct edges.
keywords.example: 1-2-3-1 is a 3-cycle; adding any edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='160' y1='40' x2='270' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='270' y1='120' x2='220' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='220' y1='200' x2='100' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='100' y1='200' x2='50' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='50' y1='120' x2='160' y2='40' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='160' cy='40' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='270' cy='120' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='220' cy='200' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='100' cy='200' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='50' cy='120' r='10' class='hi'/><text class='tag mut' x='15' y='30'>5 vertices, 5 edges: one cycle</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five vertices joined in a single cycle, showing that a connected cyclic graph on n vertices needs exactly n edges.

given.aim: Determine the minimum edge count of a connected graph on n vertices that contains at least one cycle.

terms.meaning: The number of vertices in the graph.
terms.example: If n = 5 the answer will be 5 edges.
terms.connects: All bounds are expressed in terms of n.

terms.meaning: Forces at least n - 1 edges (the tree lower bound).
terms.example: Any spanning tree on n vertices uses n - 1 edges.
terms.connects: Sets the floor before a cycle is even considered.

terms.meaning: Forces strictly more than n - 1 edges, since n - 1 edges gives an acyclic tree.
terms.example: Adding one chord to a tree introduces a cycle, giving n edges.
terms.connects: Pushes the minimum from n - 1 up to n.

given.plan: Use the tree edge-count fact (n - 1 edges, acyclic) as the connected lower bound, then add exactly one edge to create a cycle, landing on n edges.

steps.title: Connected lower bound
steps.apply: Any connected graph on n vertices must have at least n - 1 edges; equality holds exactly for trees. So we cannot use fewer than n - 1 edges and stay connected.
steps.note: n - 1 edges is the absolute minimum for connectivity alone.

steps.title: A tree has no cycle
steps.apply: With exactly n - 1 edges the connected graph is a tree, which by definition contains no cycle. Hence n - 1 edges cannot satisfy the 'cyclic' requirement, ruling out option A.
steps.note: Trees are precisely the connected acyclic graphs.

steps.title: Add one edge to get a cycle
steps.apply: Adding a single extra edge to a tree creates exactly one cycle while keeping the graph connected, giving n edges. A single cycle through all n vertices realises this with n edges, so n edges are achievable and necessary. The minimum is n, which is option B.
steps.note: Equivalently, a connected graph with n edges has exactly one independent cycle.

solution.result: Minimum number of edges = n  =>  option B

questions.formula_note: The decisive fact is that a connected graph on n vertices needs n - 1 edges and is then a (cycle-free) tree; one more edge is required to create a cycle, so the minimum cyclic connected graph has exactly n edges.

questions.question: The number of articulation points of the following graph is. [The graph has 7 vertices labelled 1-7. Edges: 1-2, 1-3, 2-3 (a triangle), 2-4, 3-5, 5-6, 5-7.] A. 0 B. 1 C. 2 D. 3
questions.to_find: Count the articulation points (cut vertices) of the given 7-vertex graph

understand.plain: An articulation point (cut vertex) is a vertex whose removal disconnects the graph (increases the number of connected components). Test each vertex: deleting vertex 2 cuts off the leaf 4; deleting vertex 3 separates the {1,2,4} side from the {5,6,7} side; deleting vertex 5 cuts off the leaves 6 and 7. Vertices 1, 4, 6, 7 are not cut vertices. So there are exactly 3 articulation points: 2, 3 and 5.

keywords.explain: A vertex whose deletion (with its incident edges) increases the number of connected components.
keywords.example: Removing vertex 5 disconnects 6 and 7 from the rest, so 5 is an articulation point.

keywords.explain: A bridge is an edge whose removal disconnects; a cut vertex is a vertex whose removal disconnects. Endpoints of a bridge (with degree > 1) are usually cut vertices.
keywords.example: Edge 3-5 is a bridge, and both 3 and 5 are cut vertices.

keywords.term: Leaf is never a cut vertex
keywords.explain: Removing a degree-1 vertex leaves the rest connected, so leaves can never be articulation points.
keywords.example: Vertices 4, 6, 7 have degree 1 and are not cut vertices.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='50' x2='40' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='90' y1='50' x2='150' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='40' y1='120' x2='150' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='40' y1='120' x2='40' y2='200' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='120' x2='280' y2='50' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='50' x2='280' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='280' y1='50' x2='380' y2='120' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='90' cy='50' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='40' cy='120' r='12' class='no'/><circle cx='150' cy='120' r='12' class='no'/><circle cx='40' cy='200' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='280' cy='50' r='12' class='no'/><circle cx='280' cy='130' r='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='380' cy='120' r='12' class='bg'/><text class='lbl ink' x='86' y='55'>1</text><text class='lbl ink' x='36' y='125'>2</text><text class='lbl ink' x='146' y='125'>3</text><text class='lbl ink' x='36' y='205'>4</text><text class='lbl ink' x='276' y='55'>5</text><text class='lbl ink' x='276' y='135'>6</text><text class='lbl ink' x='376' y='125'>7</text><text class='tag mut' x='15' y='230'>orange = articulation point (2, 3, 5)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 7-vertex graph with a triangle 1-2-3, leaf 4 off vertex 2, and 5 joined to 3, 6, 7; vertices 2, 3 and 5 are highlighted as the three articulation points.

given.aim: Identify every vertex whose deletion disconnects the graph and count them.

terms.example: Vertex 4 is a leaf attached to vertex 2.
terms.connects: Each is tested in turn for being a cut vertex.

terms.example: The triangle 1-2-3 is biconnected (no cut vertex inside it alone).
terms.connects: Tracing connectivity after a deletion uses these edges.

terms.meaning: The number of connected pieces left when a vertex is deleted.
terms.connects: More than one piece means the deleted vertex is an articulation point.

given.plan: Delete each vertex one at a time, check whether the remaining graph stays connected, and count the vertices whose removal breaks it apart.

steps.title: Test the bridge endpoints 3 and 5
steps.formula_raw: remove v: components increase  =>  v is an articulation point
steps.apply: Edge 3-5 is the only link between the left block {1,2,3,4} and the right block {5,6,7}. Removing vertex 3 splits off {5,6,7}; removing vertex 5 cuts off leaves 6 and 7. Both deletions increase the number of components, so 3 and 5 are articulation points.
steps.note: Both endpoints of the bridge 3-5 (each of degree > 1) are cut vertices.

steps.title: Test vertex 2
steps.apply: Vertex 4 hangs off vertex 2 only. Deleting vertex 2 isolates vertex 4 from the rest of the graph, increasing the component count, so vertex 2 is an articulation point.
steps.note: Vertex 4's sole connection to the graph passes through 2.

steps.title: Rule out the rest
steps.formula_raw: deg-1 vertices and triangle vertex 1 are not cut vertices
steps.apply: Vertices 4, 6, 7 have degree 1, so removing any of them leaves the remainder connected. Vertex 1 sits inside the triangle 1-2-3; removing it still leaves 2-3 connected and everything else intact. None of these is a cut vertex.
steps.note: Leaves never disconnect a graph, and 1 is bypassed by the edge 2-3.

steps.title: Count
steps.apply: Exactly three vertices, 2, 3 and 5, are articulation points. The count is 3, which is option D.

solution.result: Number of articulation points = 3  =>  option D

questions.formula_note: The key idea is that a vertex is an articulation point exactly when its removal increases the number of components; here the two ends of the bridge 3-5 plus the vertex 2 holding leaf 4 give three such vertices.

questions.question: Let G be a connected, undirected graph. A cut in G is a set of edges whose removal results in G being broken into two or more components, which are not connected with each other. The size of a cut is called its cardinality. A min-cut of G is a cut in G of minimum cardinality. Consider the following graph on vertices A, B, C, D, E, F with edges A-E, A-B, A-D, A-F, E-D, E-F, B-D, B-C, C-F, D-F. a. Which of the following sets of edges is a cut? i. {(A,B),(E,F),(B,D),(A,E),(A,D)} ii. {(B,D),(C,F),(A,B)} b. What is cardinality of min-cut in this graph? c. Prove that if a connected undirected graph G with n vertices has a min-cut of cardinality k, then G has at least (n*k)/2 edges.
questions.answer: a. Set (i) is NOT a cut (the graph stays connected after removing it); set (ii) IS a cut (it isolates vertices B and C from {A,D,E,F}). b. The cardinality of the min-cut is 2. c. Since the min-cut equals the edge connectivity, every vertex has degree >= k, so 2|E| = sum of degrees >= n*k, giving |E| >= (n*k)/2.
questions.to_find: Decide which edge sets are cuts, find the min-cut cardinality, and prove the edge lower bound |E| >= n*k/2

understand.plain: A cut is a set of edges whose removal disconnects the graph; its size is the cut's cardinality, and the min-cut is the smallest such set (the edge connectivity). For part a we delete each candidate set and check connectivity. For part b we look for the cheapest way to split the graph, which is to isolate the lowest-degree vertex C (degree 2). For part c we use that the edges at any single vertex form a cut, so the edge connectivity k can be at most every vertex's degree, forcing every degree to be at least k.

keywords.explain: A set of edges whose removal disconnects the graph into two or more components.
keywords.example: Removing the two edges B-C and C-F isolates vertex C, so {B-C, C-F} is a cut.

keywords.explain: The minimum cardinality of any edge cut, written lambda(G); it never exceeds the smallest vertex degree.
keywords.example: Here min-degree is deg(C) = 2, and isolating C achieves a cut of size 2, so lambda(G) = 2.

keywords.explain: The sum of all vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges.
keywords.example: If every one of n vertices has degree >= k, the degree sum is >= n*k, so |E| >= n*k/2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='50' x2='300' y2='50' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='50' x2='40' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='50' x2='150' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='110' y1='50' x2='300' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='300' y1='50' x2='150' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='300' y1='50' x2='300' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='40' y1='130' x2='150' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='40' y1='130' x2='80' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='80' y1='210' x2='300' y2='210' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='130' x2='300' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='110' cy='50' r='11' class='bg'/><circle cx='300' cy='50' r='11' class='bg'/><circle cx='40' cy='130' r='11' class='bg'/><circle cx='150' cy='130' r='11' class='bg'/><circle cx='80' cy='210' r='11' class='no'/><circle cx='300' cy='210' r='11' class='bg'/><text class='lbl ink' x='106' y='55'>A</text><text class='lbl ink' x='296' y='55'>E</text><text class='lbl ink' x='30' y='123'>B</text><text class='lbl ink' x='146' y='135'>D</text><text class='lbl ink' x='76' y='215'>C</text><text class='lbl ink' x='296' y='215'>F</text><text class='tag mut' x='15' y='245'>purple: cut B-C, C-F isolates C (min-cut = 2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The 6-vertex graph A,B,C,D,E,F with the two edges at the degree-2 vertex C highlighted, showing the size-2 min-cut that isolates C.

given.aim: Classify two candidate edge sets as cuts, compute the min-cut cardinality, and prove the |E| >= n*k/2 edge bound.

terms.meaning: The 10 edges A-E, A-B, A-D, A-F, E-D, E-F, B-D, B-C, C-F, D-F.
terms.example: Vertex degrees are A:4, B:3, C:2, D:4, E:3, F:4, summing to 20 = 2*10.
terms.connects: Degrees drive both the min-cut and the part-c inequality.

terms.example: After removing it, A-F, E-D, B-C, C-F, D-F remain and keep all vertices connected.
Removing it leaves only B-C joining {B,C}, separating them from {A,D,E,F}.

terms.meaning: Edge connectivity, the minimum cut cardinality.
terms.example: Equals 2 here, and in general k <= every vertex degree.
terms.connects: The inequality lambda(G) <= delta(G) is the engine of part c.

given.plan: For (a) delete each set and test connectivity; for (b) isolate the minimum-degree vertex to realise the smallest cut; for (c) note the edges at any vertex form a cut so every degree >= k, then apply the handshaking lemma.

steps.title: Part a: test the two sets
steps.apply: Remove set (i) {A-B, E-F, B-D, A-E, A-D}: the remaining edges A-F, E-D, B-C, C-F, D-F still connect all six vertices (A-F-C-B, A-F-D-E), so the graph stays connected and (i) is NOT a cut. Remove set (ii) {B-D, C-F, A-B}: now B's only remaining edge is B-C, so {B, C} forms its own component while {A, D, E, F} stays connected. Removal disconnects G, so (ii) IS a cut.
steps.note: A cut must actually break the graph; (i) leaves it in one piece.

steps.title: Part b: find the min-cut
steps.apply: The smallest degree is deg(C) = 2 (C touches only B and F). The two edges at C, {B-C, C-F}, are a cut that isolates C, giving a cut of size 2, so lambda(G) <= 2. No single edge is a bridge (every edge lies on a cycle, e.g. B-C-F-A-B), so lambda(G) >= 2. Hence the min-cut cardinality is exactly 2.
steps.note: Edge connectivity is squeezed between the no-bridge lower bound and the min-degree upper bound.

steps.title: Part c: every degree is at least k
steps.formula_raw: for every vertex v: deg(v) >= lambda(G) = k
steps.apply: For any vertex v, the set of edges incident to v is itself a cut (removing them isolates v from the connected graph on n >= 2 vertices). That cut has cardinality deg(v), and the min-cut is the smallest cut, so deg(v) >= k for every v.
steps.note: This is exactly the inequality lambda(G) <= delta(G) read vertex by vertex.

steps.title: Part c: apply the handshaking lemma
steps.apply: Summing deg(v) >= k over all n vertices gives sum of degrees >= n*k. By the handshaking lemma the degree sum equals 2|E|, so 2|E| >= n*k, i.e. |E| >= (n*k)/2. This proves the required bound.
steps.note: Combining the per-vertex bound with the degree-sum identity completes the proof.

solution.result: a. (i) is not a cut, (ii) is a cut. b. min-cut cardinality = 2. c. deg(v) >= k for all v and 2|E| = sum deg(v) >= n*k, so |E| >= (n*k)/2.

questions.formula_note: The central idea is lambda(G) <= delta(G): the edges at any vertex form a cut, so a min-cut of size k forces every degree to be at least k, and the handshaking lemma 2|E| = sum deg(v) >= n*k then gives |E| >= n*k/2.

formulas.name: Greedy Maximum Independent Set on a Tree (Leaf Rule)
formulas.latex: \text{repeatedly pick a leaf }u,\ I\leftarrow I\cup\{u\}\text{ if no neighbour of }u\in I,\ \text{delete }u
formulas.plain: On a tree, a maximum independent set is found greedily by repeatedly selecting a leaf (degree <= 1), adding it to I when none of its neighbours is already in I, then deleting it; runs in O(n).
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for a maximum independent set (or minimum vertex cover) of a tree or forest.

terms.means: a leaf, a vertex of degree at most 1 in the current forest
linear running time; each of the n vertices is processed once

formulas.trap: The greedy leaf rule is optimal only on trees/forests; on a general graph greedily picking minimum-degree vertices need not give a maximum independent set.
formulas.reference: Greedy / dynamic-programming maximum independent set on trees

formulas.name: Edges in a Tree and Connectivity Lower Bound
formulas.plain: A tree on n vertices has exactly n - 1 edges; any connected graph on n vertices has at least n - 1 edges, with equality only for trees (which are acyclic).
formulas.whenToUse: When reasoning about minimum edge counts for connectivity, spanning trees, or when a cycle must be present.
formulas.trap: Exactly n - 1 edges plus connectivity forces a tree (no cycle); to guarantee a cycle you need at least n edges.
formulas.reference: Basic tree properties / spanning-tree edge count

formulas.name: Articulation Point (Cut Vertex)
formulas.latex: v\text{ is an articulation point}\iff c(G-v)>c(G)
formulas.plain: A vertex v is an articulation point (cut vertex) if removing v (and its incident edges) increases the number of connected components of the graph.
formulas.whenToUse: When counting or identifying cut vertices, biconnected components, or single points of failure in a network.

terms.means: number of connected components of G
graph G with vertex v and all its incident edges removed

formulas.trap: A degree-1 (leaf) vertex is never an articulation point; both interior endpoints of a bridge usually are.
formulas.reference: Articulation points and biconnected components (Tarjan / DFS low-link)

formulas.name: Edge Connectivity Bounded by Minimum Degree
formulas.plain: The edge connectivity lambda(G) (min-cut cardinality) is at most the minimum degree delta(G), because the edges at any vertex form a cut isolating it; hence a min-cut of size k forces every vertex degree to be at least k.
formulas.whenToUse: When bounding the min-cut / edge connectivity of a graph from vertex degrees, or proving edge-count lower bounds from connectivity.

terms.means: edge connectivity, the minimum number of edges whose removal disconnects G
the min-cut cardinality, equal to lambda(G)

formulas.trap: lambda(G) <= delta(G) is one-directional; edge connectivity can be strictly less than the minimum degree, so a low-degree vertex caps but does not always equal the min-cut.
formulas.reference: Whitney's inequality: lambda(G) <= delta(G) (and kappa(G) <= lambda(G) <= delta(G))
